diagrams.title: Combinational vs Sequential Circuit Block Diagrams
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison showing the fundamental difference: combinational circuits have no memory (output depends only on current inputs), while sequential circuits have memory elements with feedback (output depends on current inputs and past inputs).
elements: Combinational circuit block with inputs on left, outputs on right, no feedback path
Sequential circuit block with inputs on left, outputs on right, feedback loop to memory elements
Memory element box (flip-flop/latch) in the feedback path
Clock signal input arrow for sequential circuit
Labels: Inputs (X), Outputs (Z), Memory (M), Feedback path
diagrams.purpose: Visualizes the core architectural difference between combinational and sequential circuits, establishing the foundational concept that sequential circuits include memory elements and feedback

diagrams.title: Sequential Circuit Model (Mealy and Moore)
diagrams.description: Detailed internal structure of a sequential circuit showing the combinational logic block, state memory elements (flip-flops), and the two main models: Mealy (output depends on state AND inputs) vs Moore (output depends only on state).
elements: Input lines arrow into combinational logic block
Combinational logic block (labeled 'Next State Logic' and 'Output Logic')
State memory elements (register with flip-flops symbol)
Clock signal line entering state memory
Feedback path from memory output back to combinational logic input
Two output paths: one from combinational logic (Mealy output) and one from state memory (Moore output)
State outputs (Q) and next state inputs (D) labeled
Labels distinguishing Mealy vs Moore machine outputs
diagrams.purpose: Explains the internal architecture of sequential circuits and the distinction between Mealy and Moore state machine models

diagrams.title: Timing Diagram: D Flip-Flop Behavior
diagrams.description: Timing diagram showing how a D flip-flop operates in a sequential circuit, demonstrating the relationship between clock signal, data input, and output, illustrating the edge-triggered behavior and setup/hold time concepts.
elements: Clock signal waveform (square wave with rising edges marked)
D input signal waveform (changing at different times)
Q output signal waveform (showing state changes)
Rising edge trigger markers (vertical dashed lines)
Setup time region before rising edge
Hold time region after rising edge
Arrows indicating cause-effect relationship between clock edge and output change
Time axis (t) horizontal, signal amplitude (logic 0/1) vertical
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates the timing behavior of sequential circuits, showing how clock edges synchronize state changes and why setup/hold times are critical for proper operation
